speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number nine of my interview with Eva Slomovits. Today is December 11, 1996. Eva, will you please introduce to me the people that are around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my right, sitting my husband, Louis Slomovits, that I'm married. And on my left is my sister, Lilly, that I went through the whole camp from-- and in the back is my [YIDDISH] Eliot, that I call [YIDDISH] And next to him is my daughter, my precious daughter Miriam. And that is my [YIDDISH] Jessica, and my lovely son-in-law, Stuart.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to start with you, Mr. Slomovits. Is there anything you'd like to say about your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We are very proud of her, she's a wonderful person. She's a wonderful wife, a wonderful mother, and a wonderful sister and a wonderful grandma. We love her very much. She's a special woman. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Lilly, is there anything you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to say that, in the camp, she was so small, so courageous, always hungry. And all through life, when we survived after the war, we didn't know how our lives will turn. It turned out that we hang on to each other always, when we were in good and in bad. And she sure is a wonderful person. She is special in the family. She is everybody's Saint Eva. Everybody loves her. She gives of herself more than anybody that I know. So thank God that she survived and she's with us. And hope she's going to be with us for 120. Because she's very special.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Miriam?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to just say that I'm very proud of my mother, first of all, that she made this videotape. I know it was a very difficult thing for her to do. But like all things she does, she often doesn't just think of herself, but she thinks of what's an important thing to do for others. And this is her history that she's giving to me and to my children. And we're very thankful for that. But more than that, I'm proud of the way she's lived her life. Given her history and the deprivation she had and the poor childhood she had, the kind of adult she became. And I often think that, when I chose to become a psychologist, one of the reasons is because I had hope for people, that even when they had difficult childhoods, they could really cope and come out to be super human beings. And my mother's a super human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been a member-- I've known this family for, I would say, about 22 years now. And I've been married to Miriam for 16 years. And it's a very special family to be part of and to be included in. And my-- growing up, I really had no experiences or connections with survivors of the Holocaust. And when I was brought into this family, first just as a boyfriend to Miriam, and finally as her spouse, I came to realize what a special, special group of people this family really is. They're very warm, very giving, and they've really reached out to me and to anyone who's come into this family. And it comes from my mother-in-law, my father-in-law, and the aunts and the uncles. And so today, this is a very special thing to do also, to share with us her-- their experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say that I'm very proud of my Bubbe for making this tape. And that I'm really proud of her. And she's a very special person. And my Bubbe and my Zayde, everybody feels that they are, like, the best. They're very special people, they, they always give. They always help people in times of need. I think they're very special people. And I love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jessica.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So start.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, this year--

speaker: interviewee
text: Speak up.

speaker: interviewee
text: This year I joined a Holocaust program, an honors program in my school, to learn about the Holocaust. And like, one of my motivations to do this was that my Bubbe always told me stories about what she went through and everything. And I'm very proud that you're doing this tape so you could share your stories with everybody, not only with me and Eliot. And I love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're my special [YIDDISH]. I love you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you all very much.
